element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player learns to establish stable economy before military engagement
Player builds defenses incrementally as threats escalate

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Early campaign against Duc de Puce
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Small-scale engagements before facing primary antagonists
Player learns to attack fortified positions after initial skirmishes
Player must develop counter-strategies against superior forces
Key to overcoming heavily armored swordsmen

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Late campaign with Woolsack opening second front against Truffe
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player must manage resources and attention across multiple zones
Converting enemy strongholds into player-controlled fortresses

typical_relief_triggers: Establishing stable economy in new regions
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Escalating military campaign with periodic relief through economic reconstruction and ally support; tension builds through introduction of progressively more dangerous enemies and the betrayal of trusted allies; rhythm cycles between expansion phases and defensive consolidation

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Monastery locations provide optional allied support and special units (crossbows, devices); noble allied territories offer resource advantages

difficulty_curve.early_game: Safe economy-building phase with tutorial-style introduction to fort construction; initial combat against Duc de Puce's forces is manageable with basic units
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Moderate spike when Beauregard betrays player and allies with other tyrants; introduction of heavily armored swordsmen requires tactical adaptation; Woolsack's death removes reliable support
difficulty_curve.late_game: Dangerous phase requiring multi-front management against Truffe and Volpe; forced retreat into Truffe's territory creates survival pressure; final confrontation with Volpe features heavily reinforced enemy castle
notable_spikes: Beauregard's betrayal and alliance with Volpe's forces
Introduction of armored swordsmen requiring crossbow counter
Forced retreat into Truffe's territory creating resource pressure
Final siege of Volpe's castle with King observing

notable_level_patterns.description: Linear progression through four tyrant territories (de Puce → Beauregard → Truffe → Volpe); each territory conquered adds to player's resource base and military strength for next engagement
Player captures enemy castle, must fortify it immediately to defend against recapture attempts; demonstrates need for defensive infrastructure after offensive success
notable_level_patterns.example_level: De Puce's castle capture and defense

notable_level_patterns.description: Player manages simultaneous campaigns in multiple territories; requires split focus between economic stability and military engagement
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Late campaign with Woolsack's front against Truffe and player's front against Beauregard

notable_level_patterns.description: Initial invasion fails; player must hide and rebuild strength before mounting successful counter-offensive; demonstrates importance of resource reserves
Separate campaign mode focused on reconstruction after military victory; allows player to master economic systems without combat pressure
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Economic campaign after main campaign completion

notable_level_patterns.description: Optional scenario mode allowing attack or defense of historically accurate medieval castles; provides focused siege practice
